observations.managementAction: Due to Other Funds and Due from Other Funds Accounts still exists

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Disposal Committee to initiate the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties thru appropriate modes after the appraised value has been determined by the said committee.

We finally recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to derecognize from the books the properties recorded in the books after its proper disposal.

We recommended that the officers and employees concerned submit within 30 days from receipt of this AOM, the liquidation reports and their supporting documents, and/or restitute/return immediately the amount equivalent to their respective unliquidated cash advances.

We further recommended the Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal Accountant:

enforce liquidation of the outstanding cash advances from officers and employees concerned; and

ensure that officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances shall not be granted with another cash advance unless the previous cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the books, in accordance with the provisions under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and 97-002 to avoid accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal Development Council submit justification to the Sangunian Bayan for further action,  why they divert part of the funds intended for  Perimeter Fencing at dumpsite to purchase  spare parts of backhoe used also at the said area.  

 Moving forward, we recommended that any realignment or augmentation by the Local Chief Executive (LCE) or Presiding Officer of the Sanggunian of their respective offices should first obtain authorization through an ordinance from the Sanggunian Bayan (SB).
observations.managementAction: Justification prepared but not for  SB

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management submit justification as to the necessity and relevance of the travel authority and the corresponding grant of cash advance and inclusion of the above-mentioned LGU Officers and Employees on the use of public funds. This is without prejudice to an issuance of Notice of Disallowance, if warranted.

 Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor and government officials and employees concerned only approve travels to the most necessary and essential to the effective performance of an official or employee’s mandates and functions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRM Officer to provide and appropriate funds for the insurance of the ACDVs to be included in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) under the LDRRMO to protect the volunteers in cases of injury or death  while engaged in volunteerism activities.
observations.managementAction: To be included in Budget Year 2025

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local DRRM Officer and the Municipal Accountant submit a justification, for the Audit Team’s review and evaluation, why payment for wages of the different activities  of the LDRRMO was charged against the LDRRMF even if it is not directly related to LDRRM programs and projects.

We also recommended that, henceforth, a thorough review on the validity, propriety of transactions and the completeness of supporting documents be made by the Municipal Accountant and other officials concerned.

We further recommended that management refrain using the fund for salaries and wages for job order personnel who are performing administrative functions and should be charged to Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses of the General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and other employees concerned to submit to the Office of the Auditor the foregoing paid disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents the soonest possible time for the Audit Team to confirm the propriety of the disbursements.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the foregoing COA Circular on the submission of accounts/reports to the Auditor.

We further recommend that the Municipal Mayor compel the Municipal Treasurer to submit the   original cancelled checks the soonest possible time.

Moving forward, we recommended that the  Municipal Treasurer should file or attach the original cancelled checks to the Report of Check Issued (RCI) and reported chronologically with the other checks issued and the word “Cancelled” shall be indicated on the report as required under Section 59 of NGAS for LGU.

We also recommended that the Supply Officer reconcile the balances between the Property Cards with the RPCPPE.  

Finally, we recommended that the Supply Officer prepare property cards, complete with information as to property number, etc. to be indicated in the RPCPPE, and prepare the property sticker complete with data pursuant to Item 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-06, conduct  a complete physical count together with the inventory committee of all properties of the LGU every year and prepare a completely filled out RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer and the Supply Officer submit a written justification to the audit team for their failure to prepare the necessary reports.

 We also recommended that the Municipal Health Officer record all donations-in-kind received by the RHU in the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, duly supported with the (1) Acknowledgment 

Receipts, (2) proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries not necessarily in the required form, and (3) the inventory of remaining undistributed items, if any; and submit a copy of the same to the Supply Officer and the Municipal Accountant.

In addition, we recommended that the Supply Officer: 

maintain copies of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and its supporting documents and prepare and maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.

We also recommended that the Municipal Assessor coordinate with the Provincial Environment and National Resources Officer (PENRO) Biliran for assistance on the steps to be taken for the titling of lands owned by LGU Cabucgayan through the Land Administration Management Project (LAMP).
observations.managementAction: Not all were Titled

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the unexpended balance of LDRRMF from current year appropriation before the calendar period ends, including the proper preparation of full disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements of the amounts and the details of the unexpended balance of LDRRRMF to adequately provide a relevant information in the Financial Statements.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the correctness of the utilization report prepared by the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to avoid any inaccurate disclosure of unexpended LDRRM Fund amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, prepare the Report on the Utilization of the 20% Development Fund, to be certified correct by the Municipal Accountant. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Office and the Municipal Engineering Office, undertake measures to address the risk of further significant delays in the implementation of the Rehabilitation of San Bernardo Swimming Pool, and impose liquidated damages upon the Contractor, if warranted.
 
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor and members of the Municipal Development Council, assess the attainability and necessity for the continuance of the unimplemented projects, especially those with uncertain funding sources, and consider re-programming, if warranted.

Henceforth, we also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Local Development Council and other Departments concerned, ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and implementation-ready, and ensure that all projects are implemented within the timelines set by the Local Development Council, as embodied in DOF-DBM-DILG JMC No. 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

Immediately prepare and transmit to the Accounting Office, the liquidation documents of cash advances for which the purposes they were granted have been served;

Liquidate all cash advances at the end of the year; and

Discontinue the practice of transferring the accountability over disbursements and liquidation of cash advances to third persons.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all personnel concerned continuously adhere to the provisions of the aforementioned DBM-DOF-DILG JMC, to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality are achieved and the intended purposes of the 20% Development Fund are realized.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Thru the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Treasurer, install a visible identification number to each section of the Public Market.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

Declare vacant to stalls whose tenants discontinue to operate their business, and accordingly invite (thru public bidding) interested applicants in accordance with Section 5A.08 of the Revenue Code.

We further recommended that the Supply Officer maintain copies of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and its supporting documents and prepare and maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, based on the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM, make a disclosure in the Notes to FS as to the quantity of received, issued and balance of all donation-in-kind received by the Municipality, with categorization as to food items, medicines and medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 

Initiate the request for write-off dormant unliquidated cash advances, thru the Municipal Accountant, especially to those accountable officers who have already died and/or absconded, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, only after exhausting all possible remedies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

Supervise, thru the Committee, the preparation of the Physical Inventory Plan and the submission of a copy to the COA Audit Team, at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; and

Supervise the conduct of the physical count and the immediate preparation of a Report on Physical Count of PPE using the prescribed form and the submission of the report to the Audit Team, and

Initiate the implementation of all prior years’ audit recommendations on PPE account, as well as the full implementation of the “one-time cleansing of PPE accounts” as provided in the COA Circular 2020-006.

Consider the appointment of a General Services Officer (GSO) who shall take charge of property management.

Moving forward, we recommended

The Municipal Treasurer, who is also the Property Officer record all purchases in Stock Cards; issue inventories based on approved RIS; consolidate all the RIS in the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issud every end of the month and submit this to the Municipal Accountant.

Multiple recommendations for prior years' findings including: prepare and submit required reports, implement prior audit recommendations, reconcile accounts, return unspent funds, impose sanctions as necessary, and ensure compliance with relevant laws and COA circulars.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

pay the mandated daily minimum wage of Job Order personnel as prescribed by the RTWPB for Region VIII and consider reduction of the number of JOs in the LGU to those which are considered essential and urgent;
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor initiate the refund of the amount from all persons liable thereto.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Head of HRMO and the Municipal Accountant to submit the necessary documentary requirements provided under COA Circular 2012-001 including the proof that conditions cited under DBM Budget Circular No. 2017-3 were complied with.
Impose penal sanctions provided for in COA Circular No. 97-002 should these employees refuse to heed the demand letters.

We recommended to require the concerned officials or its equivalent to strictly observe the provisions of Section 361 of GAAM Volume I on the submission of mandatory reports, relative to the use of government vehicles.
We recommended that the Management should attach a legal document supporting the payment of such benefits.

Various recommendations previously issued such as: prepare Physical Inventory Plan and RPCPPE; conduct physical counts; implement one-time cleansing of PPE accounts; maintain stock cards; return unspent funds; initiate write-off where appropriate; and implement other prior audit recommendations.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant:

In coordination with the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer, exhaust all efforts and remedies to determine the specific details on the undetermined balances of the Due to NGA account; 

Maintain a Subsidiary Ledger for each fund received from the National Agencies; and

Prepare the appropriate adjusting entries to correct the noted erroneous recognition.

We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer: 

Return all unexpended balance or excess funds to the source agency; and

Return all idle funds with the Bureau of Treasury for those funds with undeterminable Source Agency and/or its specific purpose, only after all efforts and remedies are exhausted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact and daily all collections received by the latter, to prevent from possible misappropriations, theft and loss of funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

Prepare Demand Letters, duly approved by the Mayor, for issuance to all delinquent water consumers;

Issue Notices of Disconnection to water consumers who fail to comply with the Demand Letters; and

Furnish the Municipal Accountant with the report of unpaid Billing Statements of water consumers as of yearend.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books as Receivables all the unpaid water fees based on the billing statements submitted by the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer record all donations-in-kind received by the RHU in the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, duly supported with the (1) Acknowledgment Receipts, (2) proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries not necessarily in the required form, and (3) the inventory of remaining undistributed items, if any; and, submit a copy of the same to the Property Officer and the Municipal Accountant

We also recommended that the Property Officer, assist the Municipal Health Officer in the inspection and acceptance of all donations-in-kind, and in the conduct of inventory of the remaining undistributed items, if any.

We further recommend that the Property Officer maintain copies of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and its supporting documents and prepare and maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, based on the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM, make a disclosure in the Notes to FS as to the quantity of received, issued and balance of all donation-in-kind received by the Municipality, with categorization as to food items, medicines and medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others.

We recommended that the Sanguniang Bayan act upon the matter of whether or not to ratify the Contract of Lease, within a reasonable period of time.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor see to it that the Contract of Lease should be upheld and respected should the SB decide to ratify the contract, or a new Contract of Lease be entered into with qualified lessees should the SB decide to rescind the same and immediately settle the issue on prior years’ unpaid market rental fees with the lessees concerned.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Declare as abandoned or vacant, stalls which have remained idle for three months or 90 consecutive days and cause the posting of the notice of vacancy in the unoccupied stall and in the bulletin of the market pursuant to Sections 49 and 57 of the Market Code, so that interested persons may apply for the lease of the same;

Cause the automatic closure of the establishment of the lessees who fail to secure the required permits under Section 42 of the Market Code;

Appoint a Market Supervisor as provided under the Market Code; and,

Strictly enforce the provisions of the Market Code.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor compel the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all LGU properties as soon as possible, mark counted assets with property tags and submit the required Reports on Physical Count thereon with complete details to the Audit Team not later than April 30, 2021.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Property Custodian should maintain Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, for all property of the LGU and reconcile these with the Reports on the Physical Count of PPE.

We recommended that the Property Officer should conduct a physical count a physical count of unissued inventories as of April 15, 2021 and should prepare a Report on Physical Count of Inventories. This shall be submitted to the Municipal Accountant for recording the undistributed inventories in the books of accounts

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, with the approval of the Sangguniang Bayan and the Municipal Mayor, open a separate bank account for its water system.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive to the Municipal Engineering requiring the said official to conduct of physical inventory in CY 2020 and render a report upon completion, on the water system structures and facilities turned-over to Culaba Water District and require its submission to COA and the Municipal Accountant for the latter’s appropriate adjustment in the accounting records.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other officials concerned refund the amount of ₱588,772.42 paid to MDFO without an appropriation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Renew all contracts with market stallholders including other areas for rent and revise the Contract of Lease Agreements to incorporate the latest revised approved rates and delete provisions which are unfavorable to the LGU.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

conduct an inspection and inventory of all stalls for rent by the Municipality;

Prepare a list thereof with the names of the lessees of each stall;

Submit said list to the Municipal Mayor as input in the renewal of Contract; and 

Enforce payment of the unpaid rent from CY 2018 to CY 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor compel the Municipal Treasurer and Mr. Lucahin to present the above missing accountable forms and remit the equivalent amount collected therefrom. Also, require collectors to prepare the RAAF monthly for consolidation in the CRAAF by the Municipal Treasurer and submit the same to COA monthly.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor initiate the refund of the amount from all persons liable thereto.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the donated equipment to the Property, Plant and Equipment account and effect the corresponding depreciation of the asset.



We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the concerned Department Head immediately forward a copy of transfer documents to the Municipal Accountant of all donated properties received by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer-designate ensure that the program of work and other detailed engineering designs of all development projects under the 20% Development Fund are thoroughly prepared before their inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan.

We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Planning and Development officer ascertain that all development projects under the 20% Development Plan are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC and the designated-Municipal Engineer to:

Issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance exactly one year from the completion of the project to release the Contractor from the posted Performance Security and Surety bond (Retention Money); and

Require the Contractors to submit a Warranty Security within the required validity period and stipulations, as prescribed under Section 62.2.3.3 of RA 9184, as attachment to the DV for the Final Payment of the projects.

We recommended that the Members of the Bid and Award Committee (BAC) and the BAC Secretariat ensure that all procurements were supported with complete Bidding Documents, especially the following: 

Letter of Invitation for COA Representative with the schedule of all procurement activities 
Detailed Quantity and Cost Estimates
Approved Budget for Contract (ABC)
Bidding documents and bidding securities of the losing bidder (except for Single Bidding Procurement)
Print out copy on posting of Notice of Award in the PhilGEPS, agency website, if any, and conspicuous place, certified by BAC Secretariat.
Construction Schedule and S-curve
Manpower Schedule
Construction Methods
Equipment Utilization Schedule
Construction Safety and Health program approved by the DOLE 
Print-out on posting of Notice to Proceed and approved contract in the PhilGEPS and Agency website, if any

We recommended that the BAC Chairman, Technical Working Group and Secretariat:

Submit the Certificate of Registration (BIR Form 2303) of Paula Medical Supply Trading; and

Verify, validate, review and meticulously evaluate the bidding documents submitted by the proponent bidders, particularly in the post-qualification of the eligibility requirements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain a separate book of accounts for each of the Trust Fund/Depository bank accounts that were maintained by the Municipality.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all Concerned Officials initiate the procurement of the goods specified in the Project Proposals in order to start the implementation of the Supplemental Feeding Program and Livelihood formation for Informal Sectors, otherwise, request from the Source Agency for the approval to revise the Project Proposals or return of the unused funds to the Source Agency.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate reconcile the reciprocal accounts, Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and effect adjustments to reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data, pursuant to Section 111(2) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Development Council, through the Municipal Mayor as Chairman, include only in the Annual Investment Plan the projects which can readily be implemented by the LGU during the year, through proper scheduling and prioritization.

We recommended further that the Municipal Engineer shall implement all the projects awarded during the year to ensure utilization of the 20% Development Fund.

We also recommended for the submission of the actual usage of the telephone expenses and internet expenses.  This is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Suspension or Notice of Disallowance, if warranted.

We recommended that the designated Property Officer:

Maintain an updated and complete Stock Cards for every supply and material procured by the LGU, using the same as basis of stock level for succeeding procurement and as guide in monitoring prices. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

maintain an updated and complete Supplies Ledger Cards;

reconcile monthly with the Stock Cards of the Property Officer; and

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the SWM Focal Person to implement the programs, projects and activities relative to Solid Waste Management during the year for an efficient utilization of the budget for the said program.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify the completed projects from Construction in Progress account to its appropriate asset account.

We reiterated our prior year recommendations that:

a)	the Municipal Accountant prepare Journal Entry Vouchers to cancel the stale checks and restore the amount to the Cash in Bank-LCCA. Henceforth, implement strict monitoring of check issuance and clearance periods to prevent similar occurrences.

b)	the Petty Cash Fund Accountable Officers (AOs) must submit liquidation reports with supporting documents within 30 days from receipt of the AOM, or immediately return the amount equivalent to their respective unliquidated cash advances. 

c)	the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer must conduct monthly reconciliations between the general ledger and cash book balances to promptly identify and correct discrepancies, ensuring an accurate year-end Cash – Local Treasury balance.

d)	management should expedite the passage of an SB Resolution to formally approve the closure of inactive bank accounts and facilitate the transfer of funds to the Municipal Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant/Agency Authorized Officer:

a)	identify and recover any excess remittances made for employees whose salaries were not withheld; 

b)	immediately remit any deficit in GSIS premium contributions and loan amortizations to prevent delays, penalties, or benefit disruptions.

Henceforth, we recommended that the: 

Human Resource and Management Officer (HRMO):

a)	reconcile records with GSIS and promptly communicate any necessary updates to employees’ employment status to the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) using the prescribed GSIS format to ensure that GSIS records are accurately updated in a timely manner; and

b)	ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are deducted from employees’ salaries in the payroll for the month they are due.

Municipal Accountant/Agency Authorized Officer:

a)	verify that payroll deductions align with actual employee contributions and loan amortizations before processing remittances;

b)	ensure all premium contributions and loan amortizations are remitted within the prescribed deadlines to avoid penalties and interest charges; 


c)	conduct monthly reconciliations of employee premium contributions and loan amortizations by comparing the GSIS billing with actual payroll deductions and remitted amounts. This ensures billing accuracy, facilitates timely remittances to GSIS, and guarantees correct salary deductions; and

d)	maintain an updated Subsidiary Ledgers for the Due to GSIS account to ensure accuracy in deductions, remittances, and employee benefit entitlements.

We recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO):

a)	maintain an updated database of all employees including payroll records to reflect employee movements (such as hiring, resignations, retirement, and salary adjustment) and ensure timely submission of employee records and updates to PhilHealth to avoid billing mismatches;

b)	verify that Philhealth contributions are properly computed and deducted from employees’ salaries before payroll processing; and 

c)	periodically reconcile Philhealth deductions in payroll records with remittance reports and Statement of Premium Accounts;

We also recommended that the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO):

a)	ensure all withheld contributions are remitted within the prescribed deadlines to avoid penalties and interest charges; 

b)	ensure that the total amount remitted matches the actual premiums withheld from employees’ salaries, including the government’s share and maintain accurate and complete records of payroll deductions, remittance reports, and official receipts; and

c)	coordinate with PhilHealth to reconcile any billing discrepancies and request adjustments when necessary and ensure proper posting of payments.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a)	perform monthly reconciliation between payroll records, remittance reports, and PhilHealth billing statements and identify and correct any discrepancies before processing payments; and

b)	maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for the Due to Philhealth account.

Lastly, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive fast-track the finalization and signing of MOA for the enrollment of Job Order workers under the Group Enrollment Program of the National Health Insurance Program (NHIP) for them to timely avail of the benefits that could be derived from the said program.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002.

We commended Management for its efficient and effective implementation of priority development projects in CY 2024, including those funded through the continuing appropriation especially that all expenditures charged to the 20% Development Fund were not among the itemized disbursements prohibited in Section 3.2.5 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1.

We recommended that Management develop and enforce policies and procedures to ensure that complete copies of contracts and all supporting documents, as specified in COA Circular No. 2009-001, are submitted to the Auditor within five (5) working days of execution.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource and Management Officer and Municipal Treasurer: 

a.)	adopt electronic payroll system through the Automated Teller Machine (ATM) of Government Banks for the payment of wages of Job Orders and Contract of Service personnel; and 

b.)	ensure that wages of Job Orders and Contract of Service personnel conform with the mandated daily minimum wage rate pursuant to Wage Order No. RB VIII-24 of DOLE National Wages and Productivity Commission Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Board.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer, MDRRM Officer, and the Municipal Accountant ensure that the LDRRMF are appropriated and spent solely for disaster-related activities as contemplated in the aforementioned rules and regulations.

To ensure effective functioning of the MDRRM Office, we also recommended that the MDRRM Officer submit a budget proposal to Management detailing the necessary funds for the maintenance and operation of the MDRRM Office, as well as any required capital outlay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the official responsible for preparing the LDRRMFIP to carefully plan and include projects, programs, and activities that account for the utilization of any unspent funds. This will ensure that resources are effectively allocated to address the specific needs of constituents during emergencies and disasters as well as the rehabilitation and recovery efforts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the GAD Focal Person, together with the GFPS, to ensure the timely preparation of the GAD Accomplishment Report each year. Moreover, they should comply with the Full Disclosure Policy by preparing, submitting, and posting the report in at least three (3) conspicuous public places to promote transparency and accountability.

We recommended that the Local School Board and the Municipal Accountant diligently comply with the reportorial requirements in the allocation and utilization on Special Education Fund as outlined in the afore-cited regulations.

We also recommended that concerned officials: 

a)	ensure the timely preparation and approval of the budget to facilitate the efficient allocation and utilization of Special Education Fund; and

b)	ensure proper documentation and prompt dissemination of approved budgets to key offices to prevent delays in financial transactions and program implementation.

Finally, we recommended that the LGU install weather-resistant coverings on bulletin boards to protect postings from damage and ensure public information remains visible and accessible.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer submit the lacking Disbursement Vouchers and their supporting documents within five days from receipt of this memorandum.  This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notices of Suspension and/or Disallowance for non-compliance thereof.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

a)	immediately direct the responsible officer/s to conduct a thorough tracing and securing of the lacking DVs and supporting documents; and

b)	implement stricter document control measures, such as a tracking system and accountability assignments, to prevent similar issues in the future.

Henceforth, we also recommended that the:

a)	 Municipal Accountant ensure that all Disbursement Vouchers and their supporting documents are submitted to the Office of the Auditor within the reglementary period.

b)	Management strictly observe the appropriate disbursement process.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts, Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, and record the adjustments to reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data in the financial statements.

We also recommended that the Petty Cash Fund AOs submit within 30 days from receipt of the AOM, the liquidation reports and its supporting documents, and/or restitute/return immediately the amount equivalent to their respective unliquidated cash advances.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer reconcile the general ledger with the cashbook balances at the end of each month to correct errors and ensure accurate year-end Cash Local Treasury balance.

We recommended that the officers and employees concerned submit within 30 days from receipt of the AOM, the liquidation reports and their supporting documents, and/or restitute/return immediately the amount equivalent to their respective unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRM Officer to provide and appropriate funds for the insurance of the ACDVs to be included in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) under the LDRRMO to protect the volunteers in cases of injury or death while engaged in volunteerism activities.

We recommended that the Sanggunian Bayan (SB) enact a Resolution for the closure of the dormant bank accounts and transfer the funds from GPBP-STF and PCF to Municipal Trust Fund.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant transfer the balances of the dormant bank accounts to the Municipal Trust Fund account upon enactment of the SB Resolution to avoid incurring further bank charges and disallowances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide the LDRRM Office the three (3) mandatory positions who will be responsible for administration and training, research and planning, and operations and warning, which may be augmented through any or a combination stated in the Section 5.5 of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular 2014-1 to provide adequate support to DRRM activities.

We also recommended that the Property Officer reconcile the balances between the Property Cards with the RPCPPE.

Further, we recommended that the Supply Officer prepare property cards, complete with information as to property number, etc. to be indicated in the RPCPPE, and prepare the property sticker complete with data pursuant to Item 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-06, conduct  a complete physical count together with the inventory committee of all properties of the LGU every year and prepare a completely filled out RPCPPE.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer reconcile their records pursuant to the procedures outlined per COA Circular No. 2020-006 (One-Time Cleansing of PPE account balance) to arrive at a reliable PPE balance as of year end.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer adopt the following remedies to address the aforesaid problems:

develop an effective Tax Collection Campaign program to intensify and maximize collection of Real Property/Special Education Tax Receivables.

 deputize the Barangay Treasurers, in addition to the collectors of the Municipality, to collect all taxes on real property located in the Barangay, provided that the Barangay Treasurer is properly bonded for the purpose.

 prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year, and a statement of the reason or reasons for such non-collection or non-payment and submit the same to the Sanggunian concerned on or before December thirty-first (31st) of the year immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection either by administrative action thru levy on real property or by judicial action.

Issue a warrant of levy to the delinquent owner of the real property or person having legal interest therein.  At the same time, written notice of the levy with the attached warrant shall be mailed to or served upon the assessor and the Registrar of Deeds of the Municipality where the property is located.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to derecognize from the books the properties after its proper disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Property Officer to prepare Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) upon issuance of inventory and submit Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to the Municipal Accountant

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
reconcile the discrepancy noted between the amount reflected per Statement of Financial Position and GL of the affected PPE accounts and prepare the prepare the Equipment Ledger Cards for every PPE account; and
once the discrepancy between the SFPos and GL are reconciled, coordinate with the Property Officer in the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and the RPCPPE.
Further, we recommended that the Supply Officer:
prepare the RPCPPE, every year completely filled up including information as to date acquired, quantity per property card and quantity per physical count; and
Prepare the Property Cards (PC) complete with data or information as to transfer, disposal and other information about the PPE.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer reconcile their records pursuant to the procedures outlined per COA Circular No. 2020-006 (One-Time Cleansing of PPE account balance) to arrive at a reliable PPE balance as of yearend.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the Municipal Treasurer to submit the Disbursement Voucher and its supporting documents covering the said check and record transactions for adjustments corresponding to the noted omission or   non-recording.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit to the Municipal Accountant all financial reports and documents promptly to the Accounting Division in accordance with existing regulations for recording purposes in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately derecognize all inventory items that were already distributed as of year-end to ensure accurate reporting in accordance with IPSAS 12.  Derecognition should be supported by complete and verifiable documentation, such as distribution reports, issuance forms, and liquidation reports duly acknowledged by responsible personnel.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the RHU and other custodians to maintain segregated records for LGU-owned inventory and submit timely liquidation and distribution reports to the Supply and Accounting Offices.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive require a regular inventory reconciliation process between the Supply Office, Accounting Office, and all inventory custodians to ensure accurate and updated inventory records.

We recommended that the Accounting Office conduct a thorough and comprehensive reconciliation of its Due to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to PAG-IBIG, and Due to PhilHealth accounts.  This process should include a detailed verification of year-end balances and adjustments, with clear and organized documentation for each adjustment to ensure transparency and accuracy.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Accounting Office ensure that all adjusting journal entries are fully supported by valid and complete documentation, which must be readily available for audit purposes to uphold compliance and strengthen the reliability of the financial reports.

Moving forward, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe the proper recording of transactions. This includes adherence to guidelines outlined in IPSAS 1 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 to achieve a fair presentation and reliable balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all personnel concerned continuously adhere to the provisions of the aforementioned DBM-DOF-DILG JMC, to ensure the optimal achievement of the Municipality’s socio-economic development and environmental management goals while effectively utilizing the 20% Development Fund for its intended purposes.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) Officer and the Municipal Accountant refrain from charging fuel expenses to the LDRRMF for activities that are not disaster-related or are not aligned with the programs and projects under the LDRRMF.
We also recommended that Management discontinue the use of the LDRRMF to cover salaries and wages of Job Order personnel performing administrative functions. Such expenses should be properly charged to the General Fund in accordance with existing budgeting and accounting guidelines.
We further recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer, the MDRRM Officer, and the Municipal Accountant strictly ensure that appropriations and disbursements from the LDRRMF are exclusively allocated to disaster-related activities, in compliance with the provisions of applicable laws, rules, and regulations.
To ensure the effective operation of the Municipal DRRM Office, we recommended that the MDRRM Officer prepare and submit a comprehensive budget proposal to Management. This proposal should outline the required funding for the Office’s maintenance and operating expenses, as well as any needed capital outlays, to support its functions without relying on misclassified expenditures.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the GAD Focal Person, together with the GFPS, to ensure the timely preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget and the Accomplishment Report each year.
Moreover, we recommended that management should comply with the Full Disclosure Policy by preparing, submitting, and posting the report in at least three (3) conspicuous public places to promote transparency and accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource and Management Officer and Municipal Treasurer:
adopt electronic payroll system through the Automated Teller Machine (ATM) of Government Banks for the payment of wages of Job Orders personnel; and
ensure that wages of Job Order personnel conform with the mandated daily minimum wage rate pursuant to Wage Order No. RB VIII-24 of DOLE National Wages and Productivity Commission Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Board.

We recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer and the Social Welfare Officers submit the necessary supporting computation and documentation to support their claims, including but not limited to travel reports, service reports, and a copy of the Special Order issued by the Local Chief Executive assigning them to hazardous or difficult areas, or any other applicable documents.  This recommendation is made without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Suspension or Disallowance, should the circumstances warrant.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough and comprehensive review of all claims for Hazard Pay and Subsistence Allowance, ensuring that such claims are fully supported with complete and appropriate documentation in accordance with the provisions of the applicable Joint Circulars, before processing any payment.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, in close coordination with the Municipal Accountant, facilitate the calculation of the correct amount of Hazard Pay and Subsistence Allowance to be granted to eligible employees.  This should be done by strictly adhering to the prescribed rules and regulations, ensuring that payments correspond only to actual days of full-time service rendered, thereby avoiding excess or irregular disbursements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to:
create Disposal Committee to initiate the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties thru appropriate mode of disposal as determined by the committee.
send nominees to attend COA training/seminar on appraisal of unserviceable properties before disposal for the determination of its appraised value.
We also recommended that the Supply Officer prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) with the appraised value and submit to COA for reappraisal.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to derecognize from the books after disposal has been made.

We recommended that the Municipal Local School Board and the Municipal Accountant diligently comply with the reportorial requirements in the allocation and utilization of Special Education Fund as outlined in the afore-cited regulations.
We also recommended that concerned officials:
ensure the timely preparation and approval of the budget to facilitate the efficient allocation and utilization of Special Education Fund; and
ensure proper documentation and prompt dissemination of approved budgets to key offices to prevent delays in financial transactions and program implementation.
Further, we recommended that the Management re-establish the Full Disclosure Policy Bulletin Boards in three (3) strategic, accessible, and visible locations to maximize public access to government financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) administered by the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to guarantee that any loss or damage to the same will be indemnified by the insurer pursuant to Republic Act No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget and the Accounting Offices:
identify the source of discrepancies and reconcile the net unremitted contribution as of December 31, 2024 amounting to ₱770,110.47, and perform necessary action based on the result thereof.
immediately remit any deficit in GSIS premium contributions and loan amortizations to prevent delays, penalties, or benefit disruptions.
Henceforth, we recommended that the:
Municipal Budget Officer thru the Payroll Personnel:
reconcile records with GSIS and promptly communicate any necessary updates to employees’ employment status to the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) using the prescribed GSIS format to ensure that GSIS records are accurately updated in a timely manner; and
ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are deducted from employees’ salaries in the payroll for the month they are due.
Municipal Accountant thru the Remittance Personnel:
verify that payroll deductions align with actual employee contributions and loan amortizations before processing remittances; and
ensure all premium contributions and loan amortizations are remitted within the prescribed deadlines to avoid penalties and interest charges.
Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant conduct monthly reconciliations between payroll deductions and actual remittances to ensure accuracy. Any discrepancies should be identified and corrected before processing payments.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget and Accounting Offices identify and reconcile the net unremitted contribution as of December 31, 2024 amounting to ₱8,915.89.
Henceforth, we recommended that the:
Municipal Budget Officer thru the Payroll Personnel:
maintain an updated database of all employees including payroll records to reflect employee movements (such as hiring, resignations, retirement, and salary adjustment) and ensure timely submission of employee records and updates to PhilHealth to avoid billing mismatches; and
verify that Philhealth contributions are properly computed and deducted from employees’ salaries before payroll processing;
Municipal Accountant thru the Remittance Personnel:
ensure all withheld contributions are remitted within the prescribed deadlines to avoid penalties and interest charges; and
coordinate with PhilHealth to reconcile any billing discrepancies and request adjustments when necessary and ensure proper posting of payments.
Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant perform monthly reconciliation between payroll deductions and actual remittances to ensure accuracy. Any discrepancies should be identified and corrected before processing payments.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile and verify over-remittances and under-remittances of taxes and perform necessary action based on the result thereof.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to:
ensure that all withheld taxes are fully remitted to the BIR in the immediately succeeding month, as required by tax regulations.  Late remittances should be avoided to prevent penalties and surcharges;
regularly check that the amounts withheld match payroll records, supplier payments, and other taxable transactions to ensure accuracy before remittance; and
conduct a monthly review and reconciliation between the withholding tax records and the ledgers before remitting to the BIR to detect and correct any discrepancies and confirm that all withholding tax obligations are properly accounted for and reflected in the financial reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget and Accounting Offices:
identify the source of discrepancies and reconcile the net unremitted contribution as of December 31, 2024 amounting to ₱13,005.53, and perform necessary action based on the result thereof.
immediately remit any shortfall in HDMF contributions and loan payments to prevent delays, penalties, or benefit disruptions.
Henceforth, we recommended that the:
Municipal Budget Officer thru the Payroll Personnel:
reconcile records with HDMF and promptly communicate any necessary updates to employees’ employment status to ensure that HDMF records are accurately updated in a timely manner; and
ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are deducted from employees’ salaries in the payroll for the month they are due.
Municipal Accountant thru the Remittance Personnel:
verify that payroll deductions align with actual employee contributions and loan amortizations before processing remittances;
ensure all premium contributions and loan amortizations are remitted within the prescribed deadlines to avoid penalties and interest charges;
Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant perform monthly reconciliation between payroll deductions and actual remittances to ensure accuracy. Any discrepancies should be identified and corrected before processing payments.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare an adjusting entry to reclassify the Other Structures to Communication Networks account and recognize the corresponding depreciation from the date the asset was available for operation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare the pro-forma adjusting journal entry to correct the balance reported in the Construction in Progress account; and
recognize the related accumulated depreciation and depreciation expense of the completed infrastructure assets.
We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Engineer constantly coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on the completed infrastructure projects as at year-end for the proper recognition and reporting of the Construction in Progress account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
conduct thorough verification, analysis, and validation of the individual accounts under the Due to PAGIBIG and Due to PhilHealth accounts;
reconcile the negative and/or unsubstantiated balances and recognize the necessary adjustments, as warranted; and
coordinate and settle with the PhilHealth on the unpaid premiums reported in the Statement of Premium Accounts (SPA) totaling to ₱501,226.22.
We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) promptly coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer on the updated personnel’s employment status and/or salary adjustments.  This will ensure that the records of the Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth would reflect an accurate and up-to-date information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all personnel concerned continuously adhere to the provisions of the aforementioned DBM-DOF-DILG JMC, to ensure the optimal achievement of the Municipality’s socio-economic development and environmental management goals while effectively utilizing the 20% Development Fund for its intended purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Budget Officer:
Discontinue the utilization of the 20% Development Plan for administrative expenses or any ineligible items as part of the development project.  This is without prejudice in the issuance of Notice of Suspension or Disallowance if warranted; and
Ensure that development projects funded by the 20% Development Fund are well-planned, ensuring that the specific items in the Program of Works do not include ineligible expenses.

We recommended that the MDRRM Officer submit a justification for the ineligible expenses charged under the LDRRM Fund, otherwise, a notice of suspension and/or disallowance shall be issued, if warranted.
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer allocate and appropriate for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenditures and Capital Outlay of the LDRRM Office under the general fund.
We further recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer, MDRRM Officer and the Municipal Accountant ensure that the LDRRMF are appropriated and spent solely for disaster-related activities as contemplated in the aforementioned rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer secure the applicable NTC permits to purchase, possess and operate the radio communication equipment in compliance with Republic Act No. 584 and NTC Memorandum Circulars.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the GAD Focal Person ensure that all programs, projects, and activities reported in the GAD Accomplishment Report are aligned with the approved GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough and comprehensive review of all claims for Subsistence Allowance, ensuring that such claims were fully supported with complete and appropriate documentation before the payment were made.
Further, we recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO), in close coordination with the Municipal Accountant, facilitate the recalculation of the correct amount of Subsistence Allowance, which correspond to the actual days of service rendered.

We recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer and the Social Welfare Officer I submit the necessary documentation to support their claim for hazard pay, such as a travel report or service report, and a copy of the Special Order from the Local Chief Executive assigning them to hazardous or difficult areas, or any other applicable documents. This recommendation was made without prejudice to the issuance of a notice of suspension or disallowance, should the circumstances warrant.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough review of the hazard pay claim, ensuring it is fully supported by the required and complete documentation, before any payment is made.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all the transactions of the municipality using the appropriate accounts provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs to have faithful and correct presentation in the financial statements that will provide reliable data to the users thereof.
We also recommended that, moving forward, the Municipal Accountant comply with COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022 and COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024 for the proper treatment of Semi-expendable Properties.
During the Exit Conference on May 07, 2025, the Municipal Accountant agreed to the recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Review, reconcile and verify their records concerning the noted discrepancies in CY 2024 and in prior. Any verified amount for remittance should be promptly remitted to BIR; and,
Prepare the appropriate adjusting entries to correct the balance of the Due to BIR account.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, remit in full, all taxes withheld, within the prescribed due date to the Bureau of Internal Revenue to avoid surcharges and penalties, which will be the personal liability of the LGU officials.
We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit to the Municipal Accountant a complete and accurate report of the total Documentary Stamp Tax withheld for the month, on or before the 5th day of the succeeding month. This is to ensure the timely and accurate remittance of the DST to the BIR and to avoid penalties due to late remittance.
During the Exit Conference, the Municipal Accountant admitted that the errors in recording were committed due to oversight, and committed to correct the same immediately.
Regarding the discrepancies between the amounts withheld and those remitted, the Municipal Accountant explained that these were primarily due to the delayed reporting of Documentary Stamp Tax (DST) collections. She noted that the monthly DST remittances were based only on estimates obtained by her staff through verbal inquiries with personnel from the Municipal Treasurer’s Office. This practice was adopted because the official reports from the Municipal Treasurer on DST collections were often submitted late. To avoid penalties for delayed remittance, she chooses not to wait for the said reports. She proceeded to request that the remittance of taxes be delegated to the Municipal Treasurer.
The Audit Team commented that the delegation of the responsibility for remitting taxes to the BIR was entirely at the discretion of the Local Government Unit (LGU), and proper coordination between offices should be ensured to maintain accuracy and compliance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Submit a formal written justification addressing the discrepancy between the contract amount and the bid price for the procurement of the Reblocking Beside Baywalk project. The justification should include a detailed explanation for the variance, any supporting documentation to substantiate the adjustment, and a clear rationale as to why the excess amount should not be disallowed in audit;
Ensure that contracts are executed only at the approved bid price, as determined by the outcome of the bidding process, in accordance with the provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR; and,
Ensure that no construction activities begin unless a valid and signed contract and a duly issued and received Notice to Proceed are in place.
We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee and the Technical Working Group:
Submit justifications why RMA Builders and Supplies Corporation was awarded with a new contract despite failing to disclose all its previously awarded contracts in its eligibility documents; and
Conduct a thorough review of the eligibility documents submitted by bidders, particularly the Statement of All Ongoing Government and Private Contracts, to ensure accuracy and completeness, detect any misrepresentations, and declare as eligible only those who fully comply with the provisions of RA 9184.
During the Exit Conference, the BAC Secretary admitted that the foregoing observations were due to the oversight of his staff which he failed to correct. He assured the Audit Team that the bidding documents will be thoroughly reviewed before they are submitted for audit.
The BAC Secretary, BAC Chairman/Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Mayor all agreed to the recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Discontinue the distribution of relief goods to non-calamity-stricken residents and strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 10121 to avoid committing prohibited acts in the implementation, distribution, and utilization of resources out of the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) Fund; and
Include as one of the conditions in the procurement of relief goods, the expiration dates at least six months from the delivery date to avoid spoilage or wastage of government funds.
The Management promised to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, in coordination with the MSWDO/GAD Focal person, systematically gather an updated data regarding various GAD information on gender statistics and age and sex-disaggregated data and consequently update the existing Gender and Development database.
During the Exit Conference, the GAD Focal Person agreed to the recommendation.

We recommended that the Human Resource and Management Officer (HRMO):
Reconcile the records with the GSIS. Any necessary changes to the employees' employment status should be communicated immediately to the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) using the duly filled out GSIS-prescribed format to ensure that GSIS records are updated accordingly; and
Deduct all premium contributions and loan amortizations from the salary of employees concerned on the month when they are due.
We also recommended that the AAO:
Immediately update the employee's employment status and salary adjustments in the GSIS Web MPS as soon as the HRMO provides the necessary information; and
Monitor the approvals of employees' loan applications and inform the Human Resource and Management Officer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that deductions are made on time.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Implement the monthly reconciliations of the employee premium contributions and loan amortizations by comparing the GSIS Billing against the actual deductions in the payroll and against the amount actually remitted, to ensure accuracy and facilitate timely remittances to GSIS, and guarantee correct deductions from employee salaries;
Conduct a thorough reconciliation of the unpaid premium contributions from prior years, and the premium contributions from CY 2012-2024 of ₱65,633.82, to determine whether these have already been deducted from the salaries of the affected employees and are due for remittance to GSIS; and,
Maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for the Due to GSIS account.
During the Exit Conference, the Municipal Accountant firmly maintained that the remittance of GSIS contributions for employees who were no longer connected with the LGU was due to outdated billings from the GSIS. She further explained that this was due to the failure of the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) to promptly notify the GSIS of the employees’ separation from service.
The AAO, on the other hand, explained that she had already communicated the separation of the identified employees to the GSIS by submitting the ARA and service records of the said employees. She further contended that the GSIS should have been aware of the employees' separation, given that it had already processed and released their terminal leave benefits.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
In coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, promptly remit the full and exact amounts withheld from employees, as well as, the recorded employer share of the LGU Culaba, within the deadlines set by Pag-IBIG;
Verify the accuracy of the unremitted balances and remit the amounts to Pag-IBIG if warranted; and,
Conduct a monthly reconciliation of the contributions withheld from employees' salaries and those remitted to Pag-IBIG, and make corrections as necessary.
During the Exit Conference, and in Municipal Accountant’s written reply to the AOM, she explained that she had prepared the Disbursement Vouchers for the remittance of Pag-IBIG contributions of Job Order personnel within the period prescribed under the Pag-IBIG Law. She also asserted, however, that the delay in remittance was due to the Municipal Treasurer’s failure to issue the corresponding checks in a timely manner.
The Municipal Treasurer did not give his comments to the recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Head of the Human Resources Management Office and other concerned Officers formulate and implement measures to address the delayed release of wages for Job Order (JO) Personnel. This includes setting of clear deadlines for the submission of Disbursement Vouchers and payrolls, as well as, calling the attention of concerned signatories responsible for the delays to ensure a timely processing of payments.
During the Exit Conference, the concerned officials and employees raised the issues that hindered the timely processing of the JO’s wages, such as delayed submission of the Daily Time Records. Nevertheless, they collectively committed to addressing the issue and implementing necessary measures to ensure that JO personnel are paid their wages on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive Order to establish a clear internal policies, guidelines, and limitations on travel claims for JO personnel, including the specific rates and terms to be agreed upon with the LGU.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Human Resource Management Officer to state in the JO Contract the specific rate and terms of agreement for the travel claims of JO personnel.
Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough review of JO personnel's travel claims to ensure that they are properly approved by the authorized official and supported by the required certifications and other necessary documents.
During the Exit Conference, the officials and employees who were present committed to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant:
Review the Net Take-Home Pay of the employees’ loan applications before processing any documents and authorizes the deduction for loan approval;
Automatically reject the employee’s loan application if the net take-home pay is found to be below the minimum threshold specified in the GAA; and
Ensure that in no case that the authorized deductions from the employee’s salary reduces the monthly net take home pay to an amount lower than Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00), or the updated required minimum threshold specified in the GAA.
The HRMO did not attend the exit conference. However, the officials and employees who were present assured the Audit Team that the recommendations will be complied with.

We recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer in close coordination with the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough and comprehensive review of all claims for Hazard Pay, Laundry Allowance and Subsistence Allowance, ensuring that such claims are fully supported with complete and appropriate documentation, including the supporting computation in accordance with the provisions of the applicable Joint Circulars, before processing any payment.
During the Exit Conference, the Municipal Accountant agreed to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor discontinue the grant of transportation expenses more than the prevailing customary rates in the Municipality to avoid the incurrence of irregular expenditures that may be subject to disallowances in audit, if warranted.
We also recommended that the concerned Employees refund the excessive amount on the claimed transportation expenses.
During the Exit Conference, the officials and employees present all agreed to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor submit a formal written request to the DPWH for copies of the complete turn-over documents related to the infrastructure projects in question, and upon receipt of the same, immediately furnish copies thereof to the Municipal Accountant.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant only record in the books of the LGU, properties that are duly supported with complete documentation, while ensuring that the timely recording of completed infrastructure assets in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
Prepare and issue to the end-user the required Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) to document the issuance of and establish accountability over the semi-expendable property; and,
Prepare and maintain the required Semi-Expendable Property Card.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare and maintain the required Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card; and,
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries on the pertinent derecognition of properties that do not qualify as semi-expendable property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, and all the Officials concerned take immediate and proactive steps to initiate and complete the implementation of the proposed project funded by the financial assistance from National Government Agencies, otherwise, return the unused funds to the source agencies.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor promptly furnish a copy of the MOA to the focal person responsible for the implementation of the project to ensure better coordination and timely execution.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal Development Council, together with the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, ensure that only well-planned and implementation-ready priority projects are identified and included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) for the current budget year. This will promote the optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund and ensure the timely delivery of public services and intended benefits to the constituents.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide a written justification for the discontinuance of the development projects and refrain from discontinuing any identified priority development project without valid, sufficient, and justifiable grounds, in accordance with the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 and other applicable regulations.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough review and certify the completeness, accuracy, and correctness of the Report on the Utilization of the 20% Development Fund prepared by the Municipal Budget Officer. This should be done in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer-Designate to ensure that the report includes the utilization of the Continuing Appropriations, either under a separate caption or through a separate report, for proper fund monitoring and reporting.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry to derecognize the Action Camera totaling ₱68,500.00 from the PPE account to arrive at the correct account balance.
We also recommended that the Petty Cash Custodian require the preparation and submission of the aforementioned documentary requirements from the Requestor/Payee before processing any payments from the Petty Cash Fund.
Finally, we recommended that the DRRM Officer discontinue the practice of disbursing the LDRRM Fund through petty cash, and ensure that disaster-related procurements are undertaken in compliance with established procedures.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the personnel responsible for withholding taxes from suppliers and contractors, review, reconcile and verify their records concerning the noted discrepancies.  Any verified amount for remittance should be promptly remitted to BIR.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer promptly furnish the Municipal Accountant with the copy of the Alphalist of Payees to facilitate the timely remittance of taxes to the BIR.
Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, remit in full, all taxes withheld, within the prescribed due date to the BIR to avoid surcharges and penalties, which will be personal liability of the LGU Officials.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Conduct verification, analysis, and validation of the individual accounts under the Due to GSIS and Due to PhilHealth accounts;
Reconcile the negative and/or unsubstantiated balances and effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted; and,
Coordinate and settle with the PhilHealth on the unpaid premiums reported in the Statement of Premium Accounts (SPA) totaling to ₱147,954.12.
We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) promptly coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to update personnel’s employment status and/or salary adjustments.  This will ensure that the records of the GSIS and PhilHealth would reflect an accurate and up-to-date information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Include in the CY 2025 Annual Budget and/or Supplemental Budget the appropriations for the adjusted government share to the Pag-IBIG contributions of government employees; and
Coordinate with Pag-IBIG regarding the deficiencies noted in CY 2024 on the remittance of the government share for the employees’ contributions, and remit the amounts due, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Direct the HRMO to promulgate an organizational structure and staffing pattern, and evaluate existing manpower complement;
Create the mandatory positions as provided under CSC MC No. 12 series of 2022; and
Fill in the vacant Plantilla positions, subject to the prescribed PS limitation, in order to minimize the hiring of JO workers made to perform the functions of a regular/permanent employee.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the wages of JOs adopt the mandated daily minimum wage rate pursuant to the guidelines stated above.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Human Resource Management Officer:
Approve only the Travel Order of JO personnel whose contracts explicitly authorize them to undertake official travel; and
Stipulate in the contract, the rates and terms agreed upon by the JO personnel and the LGU on which to base the total allowable amount of travelling expenses which may be claimed by the JO personnel.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive Order establishing internal policies, guidelines, and limitations on travel claims for JO personnel.
Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the travel claims of JO personnel to ensure they are properly approved by the authorized official and supported by the required certifications and other necessary documents before processing payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the GAD Focal Person ensure that:
All programs, projects, and activities per approved GAD Plan and Budget are incorporated in the Annual Budget, and any modifications should be communicated to the DILG; and
Disbursements from the 5% GAD Fund should be made strictly in accordance with the approved GPB.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, in coordination with the MSWDO/GAD Focal person, systematically gather an updated data regarding various GAD information on gender statistics and age and sex- disaggregated data and consequently update the existing Gender and Development database.

We recommended that the Local School Board and the Municipal Accountant diligently comply with the reportorial requirements for the utilization of the Special Education Fund as outlined in the cited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer (MSWDO) and the Social Welfare Officers submit the necessary supporting documents to substantiate the claims for Hazard Pay, including but not limited to travel reports, service reports, and a copy of the Special Order issued by the Local Chief Executive assigning them to hazardous or difficult areas, or any other applicable documents. This recommendation is issued without prejudice to the possible issuance of a Notice of Suspension or Disallowance, should the circumstances warrant.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough and comprehensive review of all claims for Hazard Pay and Subsistence Allowance, ensuring that such claims are fully supported with complete and appropriate documentation in accordance with the provisions of the applicable Joint Circulars, before processing any payment.
Further, we recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO), in close coordination with the Municipal Accountant, facilitate the recalculation of the correct amount of Hazard Pay and Subsistence Allowance to be granted to eligible employees. This should be done by strictly adhering to the prescribed rules and regulations, ensuring that payments correspond only to actual days of full-time service rendered, thereby avoiding excess or irregular disbursements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) reconcile the discrepancy noted between the amount reflected per
Statement of Trial Balance, Financial Statements and GL of the
affected PPE accounts and prepare the prepare the Equipment Ledger
Cards for every PPE account; and
b) once the discrepancy between the Trial Balances, Financial Statements
and GL are reconciled, coordinate with the Property Officer in the
reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and the
RPCPPE.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer/Supply Officer:
a) Prepare the RPCPPE, every year completely filled up including
information as to date acquired, quantity per property card and
quantity per physical count; and
b) Prepare the Property Cards (PC) complete with data or information as to
transfer, disposal and other information about the PPE.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Property
Officer reconcile their records pursuant to the procedures outlined per COA
Circular No. 2020-006 (One-Time Cleansing of PPE account balance) to arrive
at a reliable PPE balance as of yearend.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office:
a) Provide a written explanation for the noted inconsistencies and
deficiencies in the financial reports, including an analysis of underlying
causes and a corresponding action plan to address the issues and
mitigate the risk of recurrence;
