b) Reconcile identified variances among the General Ledger, Trial
Balance, Financial Statements, and Notes to Financial Statements by
conducting a comprehensive review and correction of journal entries,
adjusting entries, and closing balances. Supporting schedules and
detailed computations should accompany each component of the
financial statements to facilitate traceability and audit verification;
c) Optimize the use of the electronic accounting system to record, process,
and generate financial data. Full utilization is expected to support data
accuracy, minimize manual errors, and strengthen internal control
measures related to financial reporting;
d) Strengthen the capability of accounting personnel by providing regular
training and continuing education on government accounting
standards, including the Philippine Public Sector Accounting
Standards (PPSAS), as well as on the effective use of digital accounting
tools, to support compliance and enhance the quality of financial
reporting; and
e) Establish a structured internal review and validation process prior to the
submission of year-end financial reports. This should involve cross-
checking figures and ensuring consistency across all components of the
financial statements, with appropriate and accurate disclosures
reflected in the Notes to Financial Statements.

Municipal Accountant to head the Accounting Office. While the current
arrangement under an Officer-in-Charge (OIC) provides temporary
leadership, the presence of a permanent appointee is essential to ensure
continuity, strengthen accountability, and enhance the overall
effectiveness of financial management and reporting within the
municipality.

properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) administered
by the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to guarantee that any
loss or damage to the same will be indemnified by the insurer pursuant to
Republic Act No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2018-
002.

unserviceable properties thru appropriate mode of disposal as

b) send nominees to attend COA training/seminar on appraisal of
unserviceable properties before disposal for the determination of its

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the
necessary adjusting entries to derecognize from the books after disposal has
been made.

For the missing or damaged properties, we finally recommended that the
Municipal Accountant and Property Officer reconcile their records pursuant
to the procedures outlined per COA Circular No. 2020-006 (One-Time
Cleansing of PPE account balance) to arrive at a reliable PPE balance as of
yearend.

implementation of the priority development projects in CY 2024 outlined in
the including the realigned projects to ensure the optimal utilization of the
20% Development Fund for its intended purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer,
identify and include in the AIP those priority projects which are
implementation-ready in the current budget year to ensure the optimum
utilization of the 20% DF.

through a Supplemental budget, with corresponding revisions on the Annual
Investment Plan for reasons that it is impossible for the LGU to implement
such projects, hence will form part of the savings of the agency.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer, the MDRRM Officer,
Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant strictly ensure that
appropriations and disbursements from the LDRRMF are exclusively
allocated to disaster-related activities, in compliance with the provisions of

recommended that the MDRRM Officer prepare and submit a comprehensive
budget proposal to Management that should outline the required funding for
the Office’s maintenance and operating expenses, as well as any needed capital

submit the contracts and bidding documents of the above infrastructure

review the travel claims of JO personnel to ensure they are properly approved

other necessary documents before processing payment.

authorized approving official should first be secured before allowing any JO

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office:
a) Provide a written explanation for the noted inconsistencies and
deficiencies in the financial reports, including an analysis of underlying
causes and a corresponding action plan to address the issues and mitigate
the risk of recurrence;
b) Reconcile identified variances among the General Ledger, Trial
Balance, Financial Statements, and Notes to Financial Statements by
conducting a comprehensive review and correction of journal entries,
adjusting entries, and closing balances. Supporting schedules and detailed
computations should accompany each component of the financial statements
to facilitate traceability and audit verification;
c) Optimize the use of the electronic accounting system to record, process,
and generate financial data. Full utilization is expected to support data
accuracy, minimize manual errors, and strengthen internal control
measures related to financial reporting;
d) Strengthen the capability of accounting personnel by providing regular
training and continuing education on government accounting standards,
including the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS), as
well as on the effective use of digital accounting tools, to support
compliance and enhance the quality of financial reporting; and
e) Establish a structured internal review and validation process prior to the
submission of year-end financial reports. This should involve cross-
checking figures and ensuring consistency across all components of the
financial statements, with appropriate and accurate disclosures reflected in
the Notes to Financial Statements.

We further recommended that Management:
a) Provide justification for the continued underutilization of the
subscribed electronic accounting system despite payment of
subscription fees;
b) Facilitate the appointment or designation of a qualified, full-time
Municipal Accountant to head the Accounting Office. While the current
arrangement under an Officer-in-Charge (OIC) provides temporary
leadership, the presence of a permanent appointee is essential to ensure
continuity, strengthen accountability, and enhance the overall
effectiveness of financial management and reporting within the
municipality.
observations.managementAction:
The Accounting Consultant submitted revised financial statements after the exit
conference.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all insurable LGU
properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) administered
by the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to guarantee that any
loss or damage to the same will be indemnified by the insurer pursuant to
Republic Act No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2018-
002.
observations.managementAction:
Management assured that after cleansing their PPEs they will comply the insurance
of other insurable properties of the LGU.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a) create a Disposal Committee to initiate the immediate disposal of
unserviceable properties thru appropriate mode of disposal as
determined by the committee.
b) send nominees to attend COA training/seminar on appraisal of
unserviceable properties before disposal for the determination of its
appraised value.

We also recommended that the Supply Officer prepare the Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) with the appraised
value and submit to COA for reappraisal.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the
necessary adjusting entries to derecognize from the books after disposal has
been made.

For the missing or damaged properties, we finally recommended that the
Municipal Accountant and Property Officer reconcile their records pursuant
to the procedures outlined per COA Circular No. 2020-006 (One-Time
Cleansing of PPE account balance) to arrive at a reliable PPE balance as of
yearend.
observations.managementAction:
Management noted the recommendations. They will send nominees to attend the
seminar on appraisal and disposable of unserviceable properties.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the efforts made by Management in the efficient and
effective implementation of the priority development projects in CY 2024
outlined in the including the realigned projects to ensure the optimal
utilization of the 20% Development Fund for its intended purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer,
identify and include in the AIP those priority projects which are
implementation-ready in the current budget year to ensure the optimum
utilization of the 20% DF.

Lastly, we recommended that changes in the Annual Budget should be done
through a Supplemental budget, with corresponding revisions on the Annual
Investment Plan for reasons that it is impossible for the LGU to implement
such projects, hence will form part of the savings of the agency.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to comply with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer, the MDRRM Officer,
Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant strictly ensure that
appropriations and disbursements from the LDRRMF are exclusively
allocated to disaster-related activities, in compliance with the provisions of
applicable laws, rules, and regulations.

To ensure the effective operation of the Municipal DRRM Office, we
recommended that the MDRRM Officer prepare and submit a comprehensive
budget proposal to Management that should outline the required funding for
the Office’s maintenance and operating expenses, as well as any needed
capital outlays, to support its functions without relying on misclassified
expenditures.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to comply with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat to
submit the contracts and bidding documents of the above infrastructure
projects immediately to avoid suspension in audit.

We also recommended that Management develop and enforce policies and
procedures to ensure that complete copies of contracts and all supporting
documents as specified in COA Circular No. 2009-001 to the Auditor within
five (5) working days from execution.
observations.managementAction:
Management promised to submit the CY 2024 contracts and supporting documents
for CY 2024 and agreed to comply with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive thru:
a) the Municipal Treasurer:
1) Require all designated collecting officers to remit gross
collections in full and without deductions to the Municipal
Treasurer’s Office promptly, in accordance with Section 69 of
PD 1445 and Items 32 and 33 of the Cash Examination Manual;
and
2) Direct all collecting officers to issue official receipts for every
transaction to ensure transparency and provide a complete audit
trail.

b) the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant:
1) Disallow the direct use of collections for operating expenses. All
expenditures should be processed through the regular
disbursement system, with complete supporting documents and
proper authorization; and
2) Establish mechanisms to monitor and ensure the prompt
deposit and recording of all collections within the period they are
earned to reflect accurate financial performance and position.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant agreed to comply with the
recommendations. They will correct the process of using the collections for direct
payment of goods for sale and establish mechanism in order to ensure accurate and
on time recording of gross collections.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Human Resource
Management Officer ensure that travel allowances are paid only to JO
personnel whose contracts explicitly authorize travel;

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive Order
establishing internal policies, guidelines, and limitations on travel claims for
JO personnel; and

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly
review the travel claims of JO personnel to ensure they are properly approved
by the authorized official and supported by the required certifications and
other necessary documents before processing payment.
observations.managementAction:
Management assured the Audit Team that they will issue policies, guidelines
regarding travel of JOs and that claims for JO travel will be supported with the
required supporting documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that a certification from the Head of the Agency or any
authorized approving official should first be secured before allowing any JO
to travel thus, addressing concerns whether such task could be performed by
permanent employees.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the HRMO,
must ensure that duties assigned to JO workers are aligned with the official
job description and job designation.

Likewise, we recommended that all Job Order Contracts entered into by and
between the Job Order Workers and the Municipality bear the
acknowledgement made before a notary.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the wages of
Job Order personnel conform with the mandated daily minimum wage rate
pursuant to Wage Order No. RB VIII-24 of DOLE National Wages and
Productivity Commission Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Board.
observations.managementAction: LGU Naval commits to comply the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer
and other MSWD personnel submit the necessary documentation to support
their claims for Hazard Pay and Subsistence Allowance amounting to
₱372,876.41 and ₱30,000.00, respectively, including but not limited to travel
reports, service reports, and a copy of the Special Order issued by the Local
Chief Executive assigning them to hazardous or difficult areas, or any other
applicable documents. This recommendation is made without prejudice to the
issuance of a Notice of Suspension or Disallowance, should the circumstances
warrant.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough and
comprehensive review of all claims for Hazard Pay and Subsistence Allowance,
ensuring that such claims are fully supported with complete and appropriate
documentation in accordance with the provisions of the applicable Joint
Circulars, before processing any payment.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and
Development Officer, in close coordination with the Municipal Accountant,
facilitate the calculation of the correct amount of Hazard Pay and Subsistence
Allowance to be granted to eligible employees. This should be done by strictly
adhering to the prescribed rules and regulations, ensuring that payments
correspond only to actual days of full-time service rendered, thereby avoiding
excess or irregular disbursements.
observations.managementAction:
The MSWDO commented that RA 9433 or the Magna Carta for Public Social
Workers entitled them to hazard pay due to inherent risks associated with their
profession. On the computation of subsistence allowance, their basis is same RA
9433 which allows a full amount of P1,500.00 for whole month (P50 x 30 days.)
They asserts that their computation reflects the full time, month-round nature
of duties and aligns with the Magna Carta to ensure adequate welfare and
compensation for public social workers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the GAD Focal
Person, together with the GFPS, to ensure that management should comply
with the Full Disclosure Policy by on time posting of the report in at least
three (3) conspicuous public places to promote transparency and
accountability.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Local School Board and the Municipal
Accountant diligently comply with the reportorial requirements in the
allocation and utilization of Special Education Fund as outlined in the afore-
cited regulations.

We also recommended that concerned officials:
a) ensure the timely preparation and approval of the budget to facilitate the
efficient allocation and utilization of Special Education Fund; and
b) ensure proper documentation and prompt dissemination of approved
budgets to key offices to prevent delays in financial transactions and
program implementation.
observations.managementAction:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office:
a) identify the source of discrepancies and reconcile the net unremitted
contribution as of December 31, 2024 amounting to ₱1,896,867.35, and
perform necessary action based on the result thereof.
b) immediately remit any deficit in GSIS premium contributions and loan
amortizations to prevent delays, penalties, or benefit disruptions.

Henceforth, to establish effective checks and balances through independent
verification and oversight between the preparation, review, and remittance
processes, we recommended that Management consider realigning the payroll
and remittance responsibilities among key offices as follows:
a) Assign the preparation of payroll to the Human Resource (HR) Office,
which is primarily responsible for maintaining employee records,
tracking attendance, and determining salary-based benefits and
deductions. This ensures that payroll computations, including statutory
contributions such as GSIS as well as loan repayments, are based on
up-to-date and verified personnel data;
b) Maintain the Accounting Office’s role in reviewing and certifying
payroll documents, ensuring that salary computations and deductions
including contributions and loan payments are accurate, funds are
available, and transactions are properly recorded in the books of
accounts; and
c) Retain the Treasury Office’s responsibility for remitting statutory
deductions and loan payments to the concerned government agencies
and disbursing salaries, based on certified payroll and deduction
records.
observations.managementAction:
Management noted the recommendations. They will assign the preparation of
payroll to HR.

and reconcile the net unremitted contribution as of December 31, 2024
amounting to ₱7,827.93.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile and verify over-
remittances and under-remittances of taxes and perform necessary action
based on the result thereof.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the
Municipal Accountant to:
a) ensure that all withheld taxes are fully remitted to the BIR in the
immediately succeeding month, as required by tax regulations. Late
remittances should be avoided to prevent penalties and surcharges;
b) regularly check that the amounts withheld match payroll records,
supplier payments, and other taxable transactions to ensure accuracy
before remittance; and
c) conduct a monthly review and reconciliation between the withholding
tax records and the ledgers before remitting to the BIR to detect and
correct any discrepancies and confirm that all withholding tax
obligations are properly accounted for and reflected in the financial
reports.
observations.managementAction: Management noted the recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant justify the
inconsistencies of Cash in Bank balances presented in the financial statement,
general ledger, and bank reconciliation statement.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
trace the causes of discrepancies between records and reconcile the same;
maintain an accurate General Ledger and/or Subsidiary Ledger for each Cash in
Bank account per fund to correspond with the balance per financial statement; and
ensure that the balance presented in the financial statement is consistent with the
balance in the general ledger and unadjusted book balance in the bank
reconciliation statement.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts,
Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, and record the adjustments to
reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data in the financial
statements.

We recommended that the officers and employees concerned, including those no
longer with LGU Naval, submit within 30 days from receipt of the Audit
Observation Memorandum (AOM), the liquidation reports and their supporting
documents, and/or restitute/return immediately the amount equivalent to their
respective unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended further that the Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal
Accountant:
18. enforce liquidation of the outstanding cash advances from officers and
employees concerned; and
19. ensure that officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances shall not
be granted with another cash advance unless the previous cash advance has
been liquidated and accounted for in the books, in accordance with the
provisions under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and 97-002 to avoid
accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRM Officer to provide
and appropriate funds for the insurance of the ACDVs to be included in the
LDRRMF Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) under the LDRRMO to protect the
volunteers in cases of injury or death while engaged in volunteerism activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Petty Cash Custodian submit
justification to the Audit Team on the mismanagement of the PCF.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer or the PCF Custodian:
22. Observe the use of Certification of Expense up to ₱300.00 pesos only,
Reimbursement Expense Receipt (RER) above ₱300.00 up to ₱1,000.00 and
Official Receipt if disbursements exceed ₱1,000.00, as required under COA
Circular 2021-001.
23. Prepare a PCV voucher upon disbursement from the fund duly acknowledged
by the recipient of the amount and approved by the Department Head. The
corresponding liquidation shall be supported with an official receipt or sales
invoice and other supporting document such as Purchase Request, Request for
Price Quotation and Inspection Report in case of supplies and materials.
24. Refrain from replenishing petty expenses in excess of the established petty cash
fund and wait for the release of the check for replenishment before reimbursing
petty expenses, otherwise it will fall under reimbursement basis which is not
allowed by regulations.
25. If the PCF is not enough to cover petty expenses for one month of the entire
LGU, consider increasing the amount or establish a PCF for each division in an
amount where bond is not required.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and other
employees concerned to submit to the Office of the Auditor the foregoing paid
disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents and official receipts
for the soonest time for the Audit Team to confirm the propriety of the
disbursements.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the
foregoing COA Circular on the submission of accounts/reports to the Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
make sure that the PPE balances presented in the FS be supported with
updated, properly filled up Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards
Finally we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Supply Officer
reconcile their records pursuant to the procedures outlined per COA Circular No.
2020-006 (One-Time Cleansing of PPE account balance) to arrive at a reliable
PPE balance as of yearend.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer and the Municipal Treasurer
submit the justification for their failure to prepare the necessary reports.
We also recommended the Municipal Health Officer to record all donations-in-
kind received by the RHU in the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed
and Balances, duly supported with the (1) Acknowledgment Receipts, (2) proof of
receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries not necessarily in the required form,
and (3) the inventory of remaining undistributed items, if any, and submit a copy
of the same to the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant.
In addition, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
34. assist the Municipal Health Officer in the inspection and acceptance of all
donations-in-kind, and in the conduct of inventory of the remaining
undistributed items, if any; and
35. maintain copies of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and
Balances and its supporting documents and prepare and maintain a Registry of
Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.
36. Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, based on the
Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM, make a disclosure in the Notes to
FS as to the quantity received, issued and balance of all donation-in-kind
received by the Municipality, with categorization as to food items, medicines and
medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others.

File a request with COA for authority to write off dormant receivables which are
longer identifiable pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19,
2016.

We recommended that the accounting office to request for the writing off of the
questioned dormant accounts in manners prescribed by COA rules.
We recommended that the Management require each recipient official/employee to refund ₱65,000.00 and henceforth observe strictly what is allowed by law.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend and the City Accountant agreed to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the actual RPT and SET Receivables using the amounts per Tax Delinquency Listing from the City Treasurer as of December 31, 2023, and set-up at the beginning of the succeeding year the amount based on the Assessment Rolls provided by the City Assessor, in the absence of RPTAR.

We further recommend and the City Assessor, City Treasurer, and City Accountant agreed to exert efforts to reconcile the records on real property taxes in order to establish the actual RPT/SET receivables accruing to the City.

We recommend and Management agreed that purchases of supplies and materials be recorded in the agency’s books using the proper inventory account as provided in the New Government Accounting System Manual for LGU’s Volume I. We also recommend and the City Accountant agreed to record the issuance of supplies to end-users in the appropriate expense account based on the SSMI duly supported with the approved RIS at least every month.

We recommend and management agreed to submit a written justification for not submitting the monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Official Travels. We also recommend and Management agreed to prepare and submit the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Official Travels, as well as, the Driver’s Trip Tickets to support the fuel consumption.

We recommended and Management agreed to schedule the remittance sometime in July 2024 as compliance to the requirement that the City Treasurer immediately comply with the remittance of the five percent share amounting to ₱594,934.24 to the Bureau of Treasury as well as conduct close monitoring of collections and remittances. 

Likewise, we recommended and the City Accountant agreed to recognize in the books of accounts the unremitted amount by debiting the Prior Period Adjustments account and crediting the Due to NGA’s account.

We recommended and Management agreed to hire qualified personnel to execute the function of IAU/IAS as required under AO Nos. 278 and 70 to improve and strengthen the internal control systems of the City. In fact, the City Government already posted the vacancy and is now waiting for applicants for internal auditor positions.

We recommended and Management agreed to install the ETRACS in the City’s public markets to capture and record the receipt and issuances of all accountable forms to maximize the use of the system and improve the efficiency of their operation.

We recommended and Management agreed to coordinate with the Bureau of Fire to conduct fire drills to provide awareness to the building occupants in responding of fire incidents. Moreover, we recommended and Management agreed to comply with the fire safety requirements to avoid fines, penalties, abatements and closure of operations.

We recommended and Management agreed that, for ongoing projects, the City Engineer require the contractors to install and update the relevant information on the project billboards based on the current status of the project.

We also recommended and Management agreed that the agency place appropriate controls to ensure that project signboards are installed at the project site like requiring the attachment of photos of the installed project signboards upon payment of mobilization costs, and every progress billing of a certain project.

We recommended and Management agreed to fully utilize the 20 percent Development Fund pursuant to the intents of DBM-DILG-DOF JMC No. 1 by ensuring that only PPA’s which are ready for implementation are included in the plan and that procurement timelines are followed to avoid delays in project implementation.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Mayor thru the City Environmental and Natural Resources Officer implement the incentives formulated in Ordinance 292 to minimize the problems in solid waste management.

We recommended and Management, through its Solid Waste Board and/or the CENRO, agreed to properly establish the City’s composting and recycling facility to effectively manage the increasing volume of biodegradable waste and diversion rate to be within target levels as outlined in the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan

We recommend and the GAD Focal Person agreed to prioritize the completion of the gathering of date/information needed for the creation of a well-established GAD database to have an accurate and reliable basis in the planning and programming of PPAs as well as in the policy formulation, in order to properly address the existing and emerging GAD issues and concerns.

We recommended and Management agreed to exhaust all available remedies provided under the laws, rules and regulations in enforcing immediate compliance of the audit suspensions and the refund/settlement of the disallowances and charges as provided in the RRSA. Moreover, we recommended that Management avail of all possible means as provided by law to effect collection to those who are already separated from government service, and those who are not connected with the City Government, otherwise other persons determined to be liable shall be required to settle the same.

We recommended that Management require the proper liquidation of the amount granted as well as the refund of excess project funds to the donor and ascertain that liquidation reports are submitted to the source agencies because these funds were taken up as receivables of these agencies.

We recommend that the City Accountant, City Treasurer and City Assessor ensure that the set-up of RPT and SET Receivables and their corresponding deferred income accounts is in compliance with Section 20, Chapter 3 Volume 1 of the MNGAS.

We also recommended and the City Accountant ensure that all necessary entries are properly recorded in order to reflect the correct balances in the financial statements.

Lastly, we recommended that the City Accountant trace the entries made affecting these accounts and reconcile the differences by making the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended that the Management, thru the City Assessor’s Office and the Property Unit of the General Services Office, exert further efforts to locate the documents establishing the legality of ownership of the subject lots thru proper coordination with the heads of concerned departments, and exhaust all possible means to register/transfer ownership under the Torrens Title System

We recommended that the City Treasurer provides the final computation of RPT/SET, including penalties on unpaid real property, and demands immediate payment of the same in order that the government may not be deprived of additional revenues to finance other developmental projects.

Further, we recommended that Management initiate a demand from Belian Inns and Hotel Inc. for payment of their business taxes.

Otherwise, the City Government may avail of the remedies by administrative action through a levy on real property and the sale of real property by public auction or by judicial action.

We recommended that the Management make use of the free accounting system provided by the Commission on Audit through compliance with the requirements for e-NGAS installation for an efficient and effective computerized accounting system that will generate more reliable and accurate balances of accounts.

b. record the unexpended balance of LDRRMF from current year
appropriation before the calendar period ends, including the proper
disclosure thereof in the Notes to Financial Statements; and
c. review the prior year recognition to ascertain the correctness of the
unexpended balances of LDRRMF for each year and prepare the
necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct amount;
3.22 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Budget Officer maintain a separate Subsidiary Ledger and Registry,
respectively, on the transfer of unutilized DRRMF to the Special Trust Fund
per year of transfer, for proper monitoring of the fund, including the yearly
reconciliation thereof.

4.11 We recommended the Municipal Engineer-designate ensure that the program of
work and other detailed engineering designs of all development projects under
20% Development Fund are thoroughly prepared before their inclusion in the
Annual Investment Plan.
4.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal
Planning and Development officer ascertain that all development projects
under 20% Development Plan are well-planned and procurement-and-
implementation ready.

5.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC and the
designated-Municipal Engineer to:
a. issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance exactly one year from the
completion of the project to release the Contractor from the posted
Performance Security and Surety bond (Retention Money); and
b. require the Contractors to submit a Warranty Security within the
required validity period and stipulations, as prescribed under Section
62.2.3.3 of RA 9184, as attachment to the DV for the Final Payment of
the projects.
5.14 During the exit conference, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer as
the Officer in Charge-Municipal Engineer agreed to comply with the audit
recommendations.

6.14 We recommended that the Members of the Bid and Award Committee (BAC)
and the BAC Secretariat ensure that all procurements were supported with
complete Bidding Documents, especially the following:
1. Letter of Invitation for COA Representative with the schedule of all
procurement activities
2. Detailed Quantity and Cost Estimates
3. Approved Budget for Contract (ABC)
4. Bidding documents and bidding securities of the losing bidder (except
for Single Bidding Procurement)
5. Print out copy on posting of Notice of Award in the PhilGEPS, agency
website, if any, and conspicuous place, certified by BAC Secretariat.
6. Construction Schedule and S-curve
7. Manpower Schedule
8. Construction Methods
9. Equipment Utilization Schedule
10. Construction Safety and Health program approved by the DOLE
Print-out on posting of Notice to Proceed and approved contract in the
PhilGEPS and Agency website, if any
6.15 During the exit conference, management agreed to comply with the audit
recommendations.

7.16 We recommended that the BAC Chairman, Technical Working Group and
Secretariat:
a. Submit the Certificate of Registration (BIR Form 2303) of Paula
Medical Supply Trading; and
b. Verify, validate, review and meticulously evaluate the bidding
documents submitted by the proponent bidders, particularly in the
post-qualification of the eligibility requirements.
7.17 During the exit conference, management agreed to comply with the audit
recommendations.

8.12 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary
adjusting entries to correct the balances of the Trust Liability-LDRRMF
account.
8.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the MDRRM
Officer provide the Sangguniang Bayan with a report detailing the Sources
and Utilization of the LDRRMF for CY 2011-2018, which will serve as the
basis for the latter in preparing legislation for the reversion of the following
unexpended balances, so that these funds could be made available in
improving other social services, to wit:
a. for CY 2011-2015 unexpended balance (QRF and MOOE) totalling
₱2,179,954.81, declare the balance in the General Fund as savings and
as unappropriated surplus;
b. for CY 2016-2018 unexpended balance (QRF and MOOE) totalling
₱2,974,960.35, revert the fund from LDRRMF Special Trust Fund to
General Fund and declare the amount as savings/unappropriated
surplus; and
c. for CY 2016-2018 unexpended balance (Capital Outlay) totalling
₱450,730.00, declare the balance in the General Fund as savings and as
unappropriated surplus
8.14 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer promptly record and transfer the unexpended balance of the
LDRRMF from CYs 2011 to 2018 to the unappropriated surplus, following the
enactment of the Sangguniang Resolution.

9.11 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain a separate book of
accounts for each of the Trust Fund/Depository bank accounts that were
maintained by the Municipality.
9.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all Concerned Officials
initiate the procurement of the goods specified in the Project Proposals in
order to start the implementation of the Supplemental Feeding Program and
Livelihood formation for Informal Sectors, otherwise, request from the Source
Agency for the approval to revise the Project Proposals or return of the unused
funds to the Source Agency.

10.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LDRRM Officer to
provide and appropriate funds for the insurance of the ACDVs to be included
in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan
(LDRRMFIP)) under the LDRRMO, to provide compensatory benefits to the
volunteers in cases of injury or death while engaged in volunteerism activities.

11.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate and/or create the three
mandatory MDRRMO Staff positions, as stated in the Section 5.5 of
NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular 2014-1, to provide
an adequate support for MDRRM activities.

12.12 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant
reconcile their records every end of the month, especially at yearend prior to the
preparation of financial reports to avoid accumulation of variances.
12.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Prepare the necessary adjustments adjusting entries on all identified
errors identified in the reconciliation of records between the cashbook
and the books of accounts, if warranted; and
b. Record in the Cash Receipt Journal on the actual collections per RCD
reported and submitted by the Municipal Treasurer, including the proper
filling of the source documents.

1.9. We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Engineer and/or other Officials concerned prepare the turnover
documents as basis for the derecognition of assets which were already in
the possession and under the control of recipient agencies;
b. Municipal Treasurer/Supply Officer prepare the Inventory and Inspection
Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) for demolished and missing
properties in accordance with the procedures set forth in Section 79 of PD
1445.
c. Municipal Accountant record adjustments on the asset including the
accumulated depreciation corresponding to the noted accounting errors
based on relevant and sufficient documents; and
d. Municipal Accountant prepare and update the LGU’s PPE Ledger Cards, in
reiteration of prior years’ recommendation.
1.10. We further recommended that the Management carefully review their assets on a
regular basis and properly derecognize any assets that are no longer used for their
own operations.

2.9. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Disposal Committee
to initiate the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties thru appropriate
mode as determined by the committee.
2.10. We also recommended the disposal committee to appraise the current value of the
unserviceable properties before disposal.
2.11. Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary
adjusting entries to derecognize from the books the properties recorded in the books
after its proper disposal.

3.11. We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant justify the
inconsistencies of Cash in Bank balance as presented in the financial statement,
general ledger, and bank reconciliation statement.
3.12. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
d) trace the causes of discrepancies between records and reconcile the same;
e) maintain an accurate General Ledger and/or Subsidiary Ledger for each
Cash in Bank account per fund to correspond with the balance per financial
statement; and
f) ensure that the balance presented in the financial statement is consistent with
the balance in the general ledger and unadjusted book balance in the bank
reconciliation statement.

4.9. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant justify the inconsistencies of Due
to NGA and Due to GOCC accounts as presented in the Trust Fund’s financial
statement and general ledger and to trace the transactions which resulted to the
observed discrepancies.
4.10. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary
adjusting entries to rectify the erroneous entries made.
4.11. Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe appropriate
recording of transactions under funds from NGAs and GOCCs requiring
Utilization Reports.

5.8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts,
Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, and record the adjustments to
reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data in the financial
statements.

6.7. We recommended that the officers and employees concerned, including those no
longer connected with LGU Naval, submit within 30 days from receipt of the Audit
Observation Memorandum (AOM), the liquidation reports and their supporting
documents, and/or restitute/return immediately the amount equivalent to their
respective unliquidated cash advances.
6.8. We recommended further that the Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal
Accountant:
c) enforce liquidation of the outstanding cash advances from officers and
employees concerned; and
d) ensure that officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances shall not
be granted with another cash advance unless the previous cash advance has
been liquidated and accounted for in the books, in accordance with the
provisions under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and 97-002 to avoid
accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.

7.8. We commended the efforts made by Management in the efficient and effective
accomplishment of the identified priority development projects in CY 2023,
especially that all expenditures charged to the 20% Development Fund were not
among the items prohibited under Section 3.2.5 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1.
7.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all personnel concerned
continuously adhere to the provisions of the aforementioned DBM-DOF-DILG
JMC, to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development
and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality are achieved and the
intended purposes of the 20% Development Fund are realized.

8.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRM Officer to provide
and appropriate funds for the insurance of the ACDVs to be included in the Local
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) under
the LDRRMO to protect the volunteers in cases of injury or death while engaged in
volunteerism activities.

9.13. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Petty Cash Custodian submit
justification to the Audit Team on the mismanagement of the PCF.
9.14. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer or the PCF Custodian:
a) Observe the use of Certification of Expense up to ₱300.00 pesos only,
Reimbursement Expense Receipt (RER) above ₱300.00 up to ₱1,000.00 and
Official Receipt if disbursements exceed ₱1,000.00, as required under COA
Circular 2021-001.
b) Prepare a PCV voucher upon disbursement from the fund duly
acknowledged by the recipient of the amount and approved by the
Department Head. The corresponding liquidation shall be supported with
an official receipt or sales invoice and other supporting document such as
Purchase Request, Request for Price Quotation and Inspection Report in
case of supplies and materials.
c) Refrain from replenishing petty expenses in excess of the established petty
cash fund and wait for the release of the check for replenishment before
reimbursing petty expenses, otherwise it will fall under reimbursement basis
which is not allowed by regulations.
d) If the PCF is not enough to cover petty expenses for one month of the entire
LGU, consider increasing the amount or establish a PCF for each division in
an amount where bond is not required.

10.6. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and other
employees concerned to submit to the Office of the Auditor the foregoing paid
disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents and official receipts
the soonest possible time for the Audit Team to confirm the propriety of the
disbursements.
10.7. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the foregoing
COA Circular on the submission of accounts/reports to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant transfer the LGSF-Growth Equity Fund from general to trust fund book to avoid the risk of being used for purposes other than the intended purpose.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor make representations with the DILG for the approval of the proposed project.  Once approved by the said agency, require the program and project coordinators concerned to expedite the implementation and completion of the project.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize the donated multi-purpose vehicle and the corresponding depreciation in the LGU books. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Property Officer to secure from the Department of Health the necessary documents like the Deed of Donation and the Official Receipt, among others, to support the transfer and recording of one unit ambulance in the LGU books.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace the journal entries made in prior years and revert the payables owing to claimants with no perfected contracts on record.  Meanwhile, a reclassification entry should be made to correct the accounts erroneously recorded in the Accounts Payable;

We recommended that the Management re-examine and keep at manageable level the recorded Accounts Payable in order to reflect a reliable year-end balance and to facilitate the preparation of transparent, accountable and realistic disbursement and annual expenditure programs.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer provide justification as to why deposits of collections were not made within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit or deposit intact all collections to AGDB as required by regulations, to safeguard against loss and improper disposition of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant set-up the RPT and SET Receivables on a regular basis using the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card and comply with the required preparation of subsidiary ledgers to ensure reliability of the accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer adopt the following remedies to address the aforesaid problems:

develop an effective Tax Collection Campaign program to intensify and maximize collection of Real Property/Special Education Tax Receivables.

deputize the Barangay Treasurers, in addition to the collectors of the Municipality, to collect all taxes on real property located in the Barangay, provided that the Barangay Treasurer is properly bonded for the purpose.

issue a notice of delinquency to each concerned property owner and post quarterly the list of all delinquent taxpayers at the Municipal Hall and in a publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay.

prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year, and a statement of the reason or reasons for such non-collection or non-payment and submit the same to the Sanggunian concerned on or before December thirty-first (31st) of the year immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection either by administrative action thru levy on real property or by judicial action.

Issue a warrant of levy to the delinquent owner of the real property or person having legal interest therein.  At the same time, written notice of the levy with the attached warrant shall be mailed to or served upon the assessor and the Registrar of Deeds of the Municipality where the property is located.

We also recommended that Municipal Mayor mandate every employee of the Municipal Government to coordinate and assist the Municipal Treasurer’s effort in its campaign to increase collections of real property taxes in order to increase the Municipality’s income and improve its financial capacity to finance more projects for its constituents.  File administrative actions on delinquent accounts, through the levy on real property or by judicial action.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer and the Supply Officer submit justification for their failure to prepare the necessary reports.

We also recommended that the Municipal Health Officer record all donations-in-kind received by the RHU in the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, duly supported with the (1) Acknowledgment Receipts, (2) proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries not necessarily in the required form, and (3) the inventory of remaining undistributed items, if any; and submit a copy of the same to the Supply Officer and the Municipal Accountant.

In addition, recommended that the Supply Officer: 

a)	assist the Municipal Health Officer in the inspection and acceptance of all donations-in-kind, and in the conduct of inventory of the remaining undistributed items, if any; and

b)	maintain copies of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and its supporting documents and prepare and maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, based on the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM, make a disclosure in the Notes to FS as to the quantity received, issued and balance of all donation-in-kind received by the Municipality, with categorization as to food items, medicines and medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create the Disposal Committee to initiate the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 and require the said Committee to recommend the mode of its disposal.  Also, to require the Property Officer to follow the disposal procedures prescribed in RA No. 7160.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to derecognize from the books the properties after its proper disposal.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

a)	direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the general/subsidiary ledger balance with the cashbook of the Municipal Treasurer every end of the month in order to correct errors and arrive at an accurate Cash Local Treasury balance.

b)	install or establish, implement, and strengthen monitor sound internal control system required by existing laws to perform or conduct regular monthly reconciliation of cash and relative accounts

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer send written notice to the payee at least one month before the check becomes stale.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant regularly prepare the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements and coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer regarding checks outstanding for over six (6) months to avoid their inclusion in the outstanding checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that Disbursement Vouchers on the payment of wages to Job Order personnel are completely and properly supported with the required documents.  

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Treasurer ascertain that the Contract of Service of JO personnel be properly filled-up, with their signatures signifying the existence of an allotment/obligations and the availability of funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor compel the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking Disbursement Vouchers and their supporting documents to confirm the validity of the recorded transactions, otherwise a Notice of Suspension or Disallowance will be issued.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the submission of financial reports e.g. DVs and their attachments be made within the reglementary period.

We also recommended that BAC Chairperson immediately conduct the procurement of the unimplemented projects.

Each shall be notified through mail, phone and home visits regarding said restructuring options, including consequences in case of non-availment of any repayment scheme like accumulation of surcharges and the eventual filing of legal actions; To strengthen field collection by assigning more collectors and establishing collection target/quota for each. Those who repeatedly fail to comply with said collection targets shall be replaced. Collections shall, thus, be monitored to include accuracy of reported receipts of funds as well as issued official receipts. To require field collectors to investigate and prepare written reports on current conditions of each delinquent debtor and gather the important data as contact numbers for future collection; Based on the results of investigation made by collectors, management shall execute these procedures, in chronological order: assessment of the eligibility of delinquents to avail of amnesties like partial condition or restructuring of amounts due, sending of final demand letters and initiation of legal actions, the latter only after exhaustive notification about the intention to do so. Furthermore, the accounting department shall see to it that all loan dues and payments thereto are accurately recorded. The dates when the loans were granted and the corresponding repayments made shall be indicated in the subsidiary ledgers to be more informative.
observations.managementAction: The Governor has designated several personnel to check on the financial condition of the individuals who availed of the loans, and to gather the necessary documents to support their established condition, hence appropriate action will then be taken after the assessment.

1.20. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Property
Officer, reconcile their records, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 (One-

as of yearend; and
1.21. Finally, we recommended that henceforth, the Inventory Committee conduct
a complete physical count of all properties of the LGU every year and to
prepare a completely filled out RPCPPE.

Voucher to reclassify the completed projects from Construction in Progress
account to its appropriate asset account.

3.10 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare an adjusting entry to
reclassify the purchased mini dump truck to Construction and Heavy
Equipment and to recognize the corresponding depreciation from the date the
equipment was available for operation.

4.11 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare an adjusting entry to
recognize the acquired mini dump truck in the General Fund books and the
corresponding depreciation.

5.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Require the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team
explanation for recognizing the inventories as expense(s) upon payment;
b) Require the Chairperson of the BAC, to submit the aforementioned
lacking documents as required by laws, rules, and regulations for the
evaluation of the Audit Team.
5.14 Moving forward, we recommended that the:
a) Municipal Accountant record the purchase of supplies (medicines) and
materials as asset under the appropriate inventory accounts, and record
its corresponding expense upon issuance as summarized in the Summary
of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI);
b) Property Officer to prepare Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) upon
issuance of inventory and submit Summary of Supplies and Materials
Issued (SSMI) to the Municipal Accountant; and
c) Municipal Accountant and Property Officer prepare and maintain
Supply Ledger Cards and Stock Cards, respectively, and reconcile their
records periodically.
5.15 The concerned officials agreed to comply with the recommendations.

6.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide justification, for the
Audit Team�s evaluation, why pre-disaster preparedness disbursements were
charged against the 20% DF.
6.10 We also recommended that BAC Chairperson immediately conduct the
procurement of the unimplemented projects.
6.11 Moving forward, we recommended that the Budget Officer prepare
updated/supplemental AIP for the changes/realignment of the 20% DF.
6.12 The Municipal Accountant and concerned officials agreed to the recommendations.

7.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) direct the Supply Officer to comply the requirements for monitoring of fuel
consumption as enumerated in paragraph 6.8;
b) require the Municipal Accountant to submit an explanation why fuel
expenses were not supported with a Summary of Supplies and Materials
Issued.

8.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the program and project
coordinators to expedite the utilization of funds and completion of feasible PPAs
by immediately subjecting them to bidding or any procurement proceedings.
8.11 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) coordinate with the source agencies for the proper disposition of the
unutilized portion in accordance with the provisions of the Memorandum of
Agreement (MOA) between and among the parties;
b) initiate the preparation of the Fund Utilization Report on the completed
projects and return to source agency the unexpended/unutilized balance in
accordance with the provisions of the MOA between and among the parties.
8.12 The Municipal Accountant commented that the unexpended balances were already
returned on May 6, 2022. They will also make representation with concerned NGAs
since the price for the project is no longer realistic which hinders the implementation
of the project.

9.14 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Petty Cash Custodian
submit justification to the Audit Team on the mismanagement of the PCF.
9.15 We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer or the PCF Custodian:
a) limit the disbursement of funds for non-recurring petty expenses of
P1,000.00 only. A PCV voucher is prepared upon disbursement from the
fund and this shall be acknowledged by the recipient of the amount and
approved by the Department Head. The corresponding liquidation shall be
supported with an official receipt or sales invoice and other supporting
document such as Request for Price Quotation and Inspection Report in
case of supplies and materials and approved Drivers Trip Ticket and Travel
Order in case of gasoline or diesel fuel;
b) refrain from replenishing petty expenses in excess of the established petty
cash fund and wait for the release of the check for replenishment before
reimbursing petty expenses, otherwise it will fall under reimbursement
basis which is not allowed by regulations.
c) If the PCF is not enough to cover petty expenses for one month of the entire
LGU, consider increasing the fund to a higher amount or establish a PCF
for each division in an amount where bond is not required.
9.16 The Petty Cash Custodian agreed to comply with the recommendations on the
management of petty cash fund.

10.8 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit explanation why

Team why common-use supplies were not obtained from the PS-DBM.
11.11 Henceforth, we recommended that all procurements of common-use supplies
shall be with the PS-DBM except when stocks are not available, management
may resort to alternative mode of procurement in which case a Certification
of No Available Stocks from PS-DBM shall be attached.

accounts to the Accounting Section within the reglementary
period.
c) Issue a written policy concerning on time submission of accounts
by the Disbursing Officer to the Accounting Section and from the
latter to the Audit Team within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Provincial Treasurer ensure that the system, procedures, requirements under the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, the LTOM and related regulations are complied with. More specifically, the Provincial Treasurer should: 3. stop the practice of letting the collectors deposit their collections to the depository bank; instead, require them to report and remit their collections daily to the Liquidating Officer pursuant to Section 29 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; 4. require the Liquidating Officer to verify the RCDs and accountable forms of the collectors, upon remittance and to affix his / her signature in the RCDs in compliance with Section 30 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; 5. require the Liquidating Officer to verify the RCDs and accountable forms of the collectors, upon remittance and to affix his / her signature in the RCDs in compliance with Section 30 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; 6. deposit intact all her collections and all collections turned over to her by the Liquidating Officer, with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day; record all deposits made in the cashbook; and prepare the RCD consistent with Section 32 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; 7. reconcile her cashbook balance with the Accountant’s ledger balance, every end of the month or as the need arises pursuant to Section 63 (F) of the LTOM; and 8. require the individual collectors to maintain a cashbook of their respective collections and deposits, fill out the amounts and balances column properly, and reconcile balances with the cashbook of the Liquidating Officer.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Provincial Accountant record transactions using the appropriate accounts to achieve a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
